message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-10 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, on-box data-sovereignty, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use python/pytest/gh as needed.

FINDING P10-PII-1 (high, PRIV): Operator private LAN/gateway IP + interface + ISP vendor leak into committed transcripts — scrubber has no IP/network-topology rule
Location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:40-58 (_SECRET_PATTERNS) and :193-245 (_hostname_patterns); leaked corpus e.g. transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d.jsonl and .../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt
Claim: The scrubber redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle/hostname but has NO rule for IP addresses or network topology. The operator's real machine private LAN IP [REDACTED], the LAN gateway [REDACTED], the active interface en1, and the ISP/router vendor 'Peplink cellular' leaked into committed transcripts verbatim via the output of routine diagnostic shell commands (lsof -i connection table 'TCP [REDACTED]:49227->160.79.104.10:443 (ESTABLISHED)', `route get default` dump 'gateway: [REDACTED] / interface: en1', and ifconfig). [REDACTED] is verifiably THIS node's current address: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns exactly 192.168.50.134. These are a distinct PII class from the host/name/email/handle classes prior passes covered.
Why real: Falsifiable and verified: (1) `grep -niE 'ip|192|168|inet|lsof|netstat|route|gateway' scripts/capture_transcripts.py` matches only docstring/path text — there is literally no IP-redaction code path. (2) `ipconfig getifaddr en0` on this node returns [REDACTED], and `ifconfig | grep 'inet 192.168'` confirms [REDACTED] — so the committed value is the operator's live LAN address, not a test fixture. (3) The leaking file tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt contains 20 '[REDACTED]' markers (hostname/username WERE scrubbed on the same lsof lines) while the IP survives — proving the scrubber ran and simply lacks coverage, not that the file was skipped. (4) The .50.x/.99.x test-fixture IPs in src ([REDACTED], [REDACTED]) are intentional ADR-0003 non-loopback examples and are NOT the concern; the concern is the en0-confirmed real-host triple {LAN IP, gateway, interface} plus the named ISP/router brand. A LAN IP + gateway + ISP-equipment vendor narrows the operator's physical network and, combined with the established-connection peer IPs, is operator/infrastructure de-anonymization data in a corpus the README explicitly intends to publish.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

EXISTS
total 3048
drwxr-xr-x  29 [REDACTED]  staff      928 Jun 17 12:59 .
drwxr-x---+ 43 [REDACTED]  staff     1376 Jun 17 12:12 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 [REDACTED]  staff    53248 Jun 17 12:59 .coverage
drwxr-xr-x  14 [REDACTED]  staff      448 Jun 17 12:56 .git
drwxr-xr-x   3 [REDACTED]  staff       96 Jun 17 12:49 .githooks
drwxr-xr-x   4 [REDACTED]  staff      128 Jun 17 00:32 .github
-rw-r--r--   1 [REDACTED]  staff      387 Jun 16 14:36 .gitignore
drwxr-xr-x   6 [REDACTED]  staff      192 Jun 17 11:43 .mypy_cache
drwxr-xr-x   6 [REDACTED]  staff      192 Jun 16 22:36 .pytest_cache

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import platform
26	import re
27	import socket
28	import sys
29	from pathlib import Path
30	
31	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
32	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
33	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
34	
35	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
36	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
37	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
38	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
39	# for the full public-release consideration.
40	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
41	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
42	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
43	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
44	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
45	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
46	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
47	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
48	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
49	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
50	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
51	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
52	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
53	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
55	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
56	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
57	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
58	]
59	
60	
61	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
62	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
63	
64	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
65	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
66	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
67	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
68	    """
69	    try:
70	        name = Path.home().name
71	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
72	        return None
73	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
74	        return None
75	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
76	
77	
78	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
79	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
80	
81	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
82	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
83	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
84	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
85	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
86	    over-redacting common words.
87	    """
88	    names: list[str] = []
89	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
90	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
91	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
92	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
93	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
94	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
95	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
96	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
97	        if v:
98	            names.append(v)
99	    try:
100	        import subprocess
101	
102	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
103	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
104	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
105	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
106	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
107	        pass
108	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
109	    seen: set[str] = set()
110	    for n in names:
111	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
112	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
113	        seen.add(n)
114	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
115	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
116	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
117	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
118	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
119	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
120	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
121	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
122	        toks = n.split()
123	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
124	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
125	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
126	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
127	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
128	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
129	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
130	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
131	        for t in toks:
132	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
133	                seen.add(t.lower())
134	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
135	    return pats
136	
137	
138	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
139	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
140	
141	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
142	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
143	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
144	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
145	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
146	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
147	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
148	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
149	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
150	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
151	    """
152	    handles: list[str] = []
153	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
154	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
155	    try:
156	        import subprocess
157	
158	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
159	        if out.returncode == 0:
160	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
161	                handles.append(m.group(1))
162	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
163	        pass
164	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
165	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
166	    expanded: list[str] = []
167	    for h in handles:
168	        expanded.append(h)
169	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
170	            expanded.append(h[3:])
171	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
172	    seen: set[str] = set()
173	    for h in expanded:
174	        key = h.lower()
175	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
176	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
177	        seen.add(key)
178	        if len(h) >= 8:
179	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
180	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
181	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
182	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
183	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
184	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
185	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
186	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
187	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	        else:
189	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
190	    return pats
191	
192	
193	def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
194	    """Redactions for the operator's machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-MO/P9-PII-1).
195	
196	    The scrubber masked email/username/display-name/handle but had NO rule for the host the node
197	    runs on, so the production machine's hostname (e.g. ``[REDACTED]`` / ``[REDACTED]``) and
198	    internal ``*.local`` mDNS/namespace hosts leaked into committed transcripts — operator/infra
199	    identity. Derive the MACHINE hostname from ``socket.gethostname``/``platform.node`` (and, on
200	    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``
201	    forms (a machine name is distinctive). Additional internal hosts (e.g. an RDF-namespace host
202	    like ``[REDACTED]``) are supplied via the comma-separated ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS`` env and have
203	    ONLY their ``.local`` form redacted — the bare label (often a project name like ``hc-grc``)
204	    appears in legitimate paths/repo refs and must not be over-redacted.
205	    """
206	    machine: list[str] = []
207	    try:
208	        machine.append(socket.gethostname())
209	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
210	        pass
211	    try:
212	        machine.append(platform.node())
213	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
214	        pass
215	    try:
216	        import subprocess
217	
218	        for arg in ("LocalHostName", "ComputerName"):
219	            out = subprocess.run(["scutil", "--get", arg], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
220	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
221	                machine.append(out.stdout.strip())
222	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - scutil absent (non-macOS) must never break capture
223	        pass
224	    extra = [h.strip() for h in os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "").split(",") if h.strip()]
225	
226	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
227	    seen: set[str] = set()
228	
229	    def _base(name: str) -> str:
230	        n = name.strip()
231	        return n[: -len(".local")] if n.lower().endswith(".local") else n
232	
233	    for name in machine:  # machine hostname → redact BARE + .local (distinctive identity)
234	        b = _base(name)
235	        if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
236	            continue
237	        seen.add(b.lower())
238	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
239	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
240	        b = _base(name)
241	        if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:
242	            continue
243	        seen.add("local:" + b.lower())
244	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"\.local\b", re.IGNORECASE))
245	    return pats
246	
247	
248	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
249	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
250	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
251	_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()
252	
253	
254	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
255	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
256	        if pat.groups == 2:
257	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
258	        elif pat.groups == 1:
259	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
260	        else:

content.text: Confirmed: node en0 IP is [REDACTED], and there is no IP/network redaction code. Now let me verify the IP actually leaked into committed transcripts.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
echo "=== committed files containing [REDACTED] ==="
git grep -l "[REDACTED]" -- transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | head
echo "=== gateway [REDACTED] ==="
git grep -l "[REDACTED]\b" -- transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | head
echo "=== peplink ==="
git grep -liE "peplink" -- transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | head

content.content:
=== README transcript intent ===
14:baseline toolkit), an append-only hash-chained provenance trail, and a single composed runner —
23:> **Not yet on PyPI.** Until the package is published, install from source (see
47:result.provenance        # append-only, hash-chained audit trail (verified before return)
118:| [docs/GOVERNANCE.md](docs/GOVERNANCE.md) | Gates, pre-registration, provenance, independence, data sovereignty |
=== transcripts/README ===
# Transcripts (provenance)

This directory captures the **Claude session transcripts** behind the work in this repository,
so the provenance and effort of the agent interactions can be reviewed and audited alongside the
code.

## What's here

- `sessions/<session-id>.jsonl` — the main session transcript (one JSON object per line: prompts,
  tool calls, tool results, model output).
- `sessions/<session-id>/subagents/*.jsonl` — transcripts of subagents spawned in that session.
- `sessions/<session-id>/workflows/...` — workflow scripts and their subagent transcripts.
- `MANIFEST.json` — what was captured, when, and a SHA-256 of each file.

## How it's captured

A committed git hook (`.githooks/pre-commit`, enabled via `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`)
runs `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` before every commit, which snapshots the **active** session
(the most-recently-modified transcript) and its subtree into `sessions/`, then stages it. So
**every commit pulls in an up-to-date transcript snapshot.**

To enable the hook on a fresh clone: `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`.

## Scope & caveats (read before publishing)

- Credential-shaped secrets (API keys, OAuth/bearer tokens) are **redacted** on capture.
- Transcripts still contain **local filesystem paths** and may contain the **operator's email**
  and internal reasoning. They are legitimate provenance, but if this repository is made
  **public**, review this directory first — consider scrubbing further or excluding it
  (`git rm -r --cached transcripts/` + a `.gitignore` entry).
- These files are **not** shipped in the built wheel (only `src/assay_engine` is packaged).
- Transcripts are append-mostly JSONL, so they delta-compress well in git history.
=== is [REDACTED] a src test fixture? ===

content.text:
All claims verified. The finding holds.

Let me note the one nuance: the README's "Scope & caveats (read before publishing)" section explicitly says transcripts "may contain... local filesystem paths and... the operator's email" and advises review/scrubbing before public release. This is a documented honest-scope caveat. However, the finding is about an active scrubber that *does* run automatically on every commit (it scrubbed 20 items in this very file, including hostname/username on the same lsof lines) and has a documented, expanding list of PII classes it claims to cover — yet has a genuine coverage gap for a verifiably-live PII class. The scrubber's docstring frames itself as the defense; prior passes (#F-024, #J-002, #J-008, #P9-PII-1) treated each new PII class as a real defect to fix. This is a consistent, distinct PII class (network topology) not covered.

The "honest-scope" defense does not fully neutralize this because the scrubber is the active mitigation and it advertises coverage of operator PII classes; this is a real gap in that mitigation, consistent with how every prior PII pass was adjudicated as a valid finding.

input.reasoning:
Finding STANDS. Every falsifiable claim verified against the actual code and committed corpus:

1. NO IP/network-topology redaction rule: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:40-58 (_SECRET_PATTERNS) covers sk-ant/gh/AWS/bearer tokens, env-var secrets, email, /Users|/home username, and project-slug username. Lines 61-245 add username (_username_pattern), display-name (_display_name_patterns), repo-handle (_repo_handle_patterns), and hostname (_hostname_patterns). There is NO regex for IPv4 addresses, gateways, interfaces, or vendor names. Grep for ip/192/168/inet/lsof/gateway/peplink matched only docstring/code-structure text, no redaction path.

2. The leaked IP is THIS live node's address: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns exactly 192.168.50.134.

3. The IP and topology leaked verbatim into COMMITTED (git-tracked) transcripts. `git grep -l "[REDACTED]" -- transcripts/` returns 4 tracked files including the named transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-...jsonl and .../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt. Gateway [REDACTED] and "Peplink" also present and tracked.

4. Scrubber-ran-but-IP-survived confirmed: bv1ufu6gh.txt contains 20 [REDACTED] markers (hostname/username were scrubbed) while [REDACTED] and [REDACTED] survive (1 occurrence each in that file). Adjacent lines show /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc and -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc redactions on the same diagnostic output — proving the scrubber executed and merely lacks coverage, not that the file was skipped.

5. Not a test fixture: `git grep [REDACTED] -- src/ tests/` returns nothing; the value appears only in the live-host diagnostic transcript output.

6. README/transcripts/README.md explicitly intend to publish transcripts as provenance.

Defenses considered and rejected: (a) "documented honest-scope" — transcripts/README does caveat local paths and email, but the scrubber is the ACTIVE automatic mitigation that advertises operator-PII coverage and is extended every pass; this is a genuine coverage gap in that mitigation. Network topology (LAN IP + gateway + interface + ISP/router vendor) is a distinct PII class never covered. (b) "test-fixture IPs" — the finding correctly pre-empts this; the en0-confirmed live triple is the concern, not the ADR-0003 examples. Every prior PII pass (#F-024 email/username, #J-002 display-name, #J-008 handle, #P9-PII-1 hostname) was adjudicated a valid defect under the identical pattern; this is consistent. Finding holds.

content.text:
Finding STANDS. I verified every falsifiable claim against the actual code and the committed corpus:

- scripts/capture_transcripts.py has no IPv4/gateway/interface/vendor redaction rule (only token/email/username/display-name/handle/hostname rules).
- `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns [REDACTED] — the leaked value is this live node's address.
- 4 git-tracked transcript files contain [REDACTED]; gateway [REDACTED] and "Peplink" are also tracked.
- transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt has 20 [REDACTED] markers (proving the scrubber ran) while the IP survives, with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc on adjacent lines — coverage gap, not a skipped file.
- The value is not a src/tests fixture.

The "documented honest-scope" caveat does not refute it: the scrubber is the active per-commit mitigation that advertises operator-PII coverage and was extended in every prior pass for exactly this class of defect (email, username, display-name, handle, hostname). Network topology is a consistent, distinct, uncovered PII class.
